questions.question: Which one of the following quantifiers is used to denote the existence of at least one element in a set?
questions.explanation: The quantifier ∃ (exist) is used to denote the existence of at least one element in a set.

questions.question: Which statement is an example of the use of the universal quantifier (∀)?
options: All students in this class are good at math.
Some students in this class are good at math.
The teacher is good at math.
questions.explanation: The statement 'All students in this class are good at math.' uses the universal quantifier (∀) to denote that all students in the class are good at math.

questions.question: Which one of the following statements is an example of the use of the existential quantifier (∃)?
options: All students in this class are good at math.
Some students in this class are good at math.
The teacher is good at math.
questions.explanation: The statement 'Some students in this class are good at math.' uses the existential quantifier (∃) to denote that there exists at least one student in the class who is good at math.

questions.question: Which quantifier is used to denote the sum of all elements in a set?
questions.explanation: The symbol ∑ (sum) is used to denote the sum of all elements in a set.

questions.question: Which statement is an example of the use of the intersection operator (∩)?
options: All students in this class are good at math.
Some students in this class are good at math.
The teacher is good at math.
questions.explanation: The statement 'All students in this class are good at math.' uses the intersection operator (∩) to denote the intersection of two sets, which is the set of elements that are common to both sets.
